text:
opinion|Letters to the Editor

ByLetters to the Editor



Re: “Courage or cowardice? America needs elected officials who stand their ground when the chips are down,” by Alan W. Steelman, Sunday Opinion.

This well-written op-ed reminded us of many who served our government with principle and strength of conscience to stand for a higher cause than their own welfare or careers. Donald Trump is not such a person. His well-documented history makes it clear that he will not be limited by morals, or even the laws most of us must obey, to accomplish his self-serving agenda. The Supreme Court has granted him full range to do as he pleases.

As a self-professed conservative, I would not normally choose Kamala Harris as president. But we, unlike in Russia or Venezuela for example, have free and fair elections, and we have a choice. I expect her to spend more money on some things I think people should do for themselves, and be more considerate of immigrants’ welfare, and maybe increase our taxes, for example.

We will survive that and more “liberal fluff” until the next election when hopefully we can make a better choice. I am going to assume that (unlike her opponent) she believes in the integrity of our elections and the peaceful transfer of power. Trump makes it a pretty low bar for his opponents to be seen as the better choice.

Get smart opinions on the topics North Texans care about.

Or with:

By signing up, you agree to ourTerms of ServiceandPrivacy Policy.

Robert Rathmell, Murphy

To Mr. Steelman, will you comment on the Americans, or the American people, as it is common to say nowadays, who voted for these representatives of ours? What on earth led us to have so many voters led astray? And please be sincere, like you were in your illuminating op-ed.

Sal Esposito, Frisco

I’d like to thank Steelman for his excellent discussion and representation of courage or cowardice. I hope he writes a related sequel on honor and hypocrisy.

Stephen Hoffner, Dallas

Re: “LDS temple a good neighbor,” by David Haymes, Monday Letters.

I would invite Haymes to Fairview to see what the issue is with the proposed LDS temple in Fairview. The proposed temple building is 65 feet high, 45,000 square feet, with a 109-foot spire placed on top — going up to 174 feet, taller than our water towers.

The proposed temple in Fairview is nothing like the temple in Dallas, which I have driven by multiple times. The proposed temple in Fairview is a gigantic building that would be located in our residential zone that has a maximum height of a house allowed of 35 feet.

Drive to Fairview’s Town Hall (which is in our commercial area) and use it for comparison. Our Town Hall is 65 feet high and 22,000 square feet. The proposed temple building is literally more than twice the size of our Town Hall — with the 109 foot spire stuck on top.

Now imagine that building in your neighborhood. It would stick out like a sore thumb.

Henry Lessner, mayor of Fairview

When I wrote my letter describing the LDS temple in our neighborhood and my puzzlement about the controversy in Fairview, I was not aware that the church is proposing a steeple of 174 feet. That does seem a bit much and I hope they can compromise on a steeple more in keeping with the temple in our neighborhood.

David Haymes, North Dallas

Apparently some vocal proponents of the current pickleball craze have mounted a rather high-pressure campaign to eliminate facilities for other users at Cole Park, a popular neighborhood public park.Dallas Morning Newsstaff seem to have accommodated this PR blitz with several Metro front-page stories and photo spreads.

What is the editorial basis forThe Newsbasically giving the thumbs up to one special interest group’s decision to mob this one specific public park? Dallas County has 2.7 million people occupying around 900 square miles. Surely there are additional places for picklers to congregate and other more representative city issues forThe Newsto cover.

Anne McDonald, Dallas

Just a quick note to express how much I have appreciated Kevin Sherrington’s Olympic reporting. His writing style really grabbed my attention and kept me interested. I felt like I was right there watching the action in person.

I usually read his articles before seeing the events myself, but that made it only all the more exciting to watch the recordings after the fact, equipped with all the insider details and knowledge provided by Kevin. Great job!

Robert Choate, Little Elm

Re: “Remembering local civil rights allies — Dallas was not friendly to the movement, but there were some shining lights,” by Peter Johnson, Sunday Opinion.

Thank you for adding Peter Johnson as a contributing columnist. He is an essential voice from our community. First, he’s been on the front lines of civil rights activism here in Dallas for decades. He is a gifted truth teller.

His latest column is a splendid example. He drew attention to the late Bishop Mark Herbener, who was a courageous voice for justice when that was dangerous in Dallas.

And then he gave much overdue attention to Sister Patricia Ridgley, who is also a champion of justice in Dallas. Both are great examples of people of faith doing courageous things.

In a time requiring courage to stand up for civil rights, it’s extremely important to hear the stories of people like the Rev. Peter Johnson, who has been doing this work his entire life. Thank you for adding his voice here.

Bill Holston, Dallas/Casa View

title: Letters to the Editor — Fair elections, Mormon temple, Cole Park, Olympics, new columnist
